input_statement: You should minimize the use of Visual C++ Runtime.
Functions malloc, memcpy, str* should be avoided.
WinAPI already has everything you need - instead of malloc - HeapAlloc, instead of memcpy - CopyMemory, instead of strstr - StrStr (shlwapi.h/.lib).
CRT adds quite a lot of unnecessary code to the binary.
C: to the binary

input_statement: 1.1 The STL should generally be avoided.
C: generally

input_statement: Some STL chips give extra prologue to the code.
It is not visible in your code, but it is there, and will behave strangely in the context of execution!
2. linking of rantime and third-party libraries to the module should be static (flag /MT).
B: runtime and third-party libraries to the module
C: static, flag /MT

input_statement: 3. Obfuscate strings with Andrivet library (attached, see _
4. Obfuscate system calls with GetApi.h library.
Be careful, obfuscation of system calls may cause crashes.
C: may cause crashes

input_statement: 1. Must have a Microsoft Visual Studio project of version 2015 or higher.
C: of version 2015 or higher

input_statement: The Visual Studio project must be configured as follows:
- General / Windows SDK version: <inherit from parent or project defaults>
- C/C++ / Code Creation / Runtime Library: Multithreaded or Multithreaded Debugging (Release/Debug respectively)
- C/C++ / Code Creation / Create Image with Update: No
- C/C++ / Code Creation / Enable C/C++ Exceptions: None
- C/C++ / Code Creation / Enable security check: none
- C/C++ / Optimization / Omit frame pointers: yes
Clang adds a string with the path to the .pdb symbol file, even if this is disabled by the build options.

input_statement: - Composer -> Command Line, specify /pdbaltpath:<string>, instead of the path to the pdb will be this string.
The module must have two versions - x32- and x64-bit.

input_statement: The module must be compiled statically and be a single .exe/.dll file.
For .dll modules, it is acceptable to include a _test_ launcher as a .exe.

input_statement: The combat build should have lines obfuscated to the maximum, and all debugging output disabled.

C: of the module

input_statement: Each log entry must contain a timestamp accurate to the second.

C: in the project with all global settings (paths, macros-switches for conditional compilation, etc.)

input_statement: The module should work on all modern versions of Windows.
C: on all modern versions of Windows

- check for unobfuscated strings in Release_nologs binary files.

input_statement: - check if there is no reference to debugging information in Release_nologs

input_statement: - files with logins, passwords, URLs and IP addresses, names, addresses, phone numbers of anyone you know, as well as their hashes.

Why do you need version control systems (and git in particular)?
- as a means of backup (protects against losing a day's work)
- as a means of centralized code storage and mobility (access from anywhere and from any machine)
C: as a means of centralized code storage and mobility, access from anywhere and from any machine

input_statement: - memory and history (answer to the question:
WHY THIS DAMN STORY WAS WRITTEN)
Believe me - in six months at most you won't remember why that fucking line was written.
If you got the project after someone else - it's mission impossible.
Almost all modern version control tools have a blame function to solve this problem.
will show the author, date and commit number of each line of the specified file.
B: author, date and commit number of each line of the specified file

input_statement: And all this is integrated into Visual Studio
(right-click on the file in Solution Explorer -> Blame (notes)).
In order to see something clear there, instead of "some update", you should
C: in order to see something clear there, instead of some update

input_statement: - commit a couple of lines at a time, instead of committing a huge bundle of files at the end of the week with a "." comment;
C: at a time, instead of committing a huge bundle of files at the end of the week with a '.' comment

input_statement: - commit only those files that were affected by the fix (instead of hacking the whole directory with the comment "commit", what's the big deal,
whatever, it doesn't matter that old forgotten crap that's been in the project for god knows how long has been there)
- use the diff tool (or rather its visual analog, such as the merge tool built into Visual Studio) when creating a commit
C: when creating a commit

input_statement: It follows that you should commit often:
- fixed a bug - immediately commit only the files related to it with the comment "fixed the stack overflow bug in function so-and-so for this reason and that reason".
- wrote a feature - immediately commit only the files concerning it with the comment "implementation of non-interactive HTTP-client".
And then, looking up the history of changes in the file, you will see the reason why this line was written, as well as the RELATIONSHIP of this line with other parts of the code.
Of course, you may not do all this because you are too lazy to study, and all that.
